identified_error: The study used misaligned/unequal baseline time points for comparison. The monotherapy group's weight loss was measured from the initiation of GLP-1 analogue therapy (true baseline), while the combination therapy group's weight loss was measured from the point when bupropion/naltrexone was added on — not from the same starting point. This means the combination group had already been on GLP-1 therapy for a variable period before their 'baseline' was set, and their prior weight change on GLP-1 monotherapy was excluded from the comparison. Furthermore, stratifying the combination group into responders and non-responders based on their initial GLP-1 response introduces regression to the mean: non-responders who had lost <5% on GLP-1 alone were likely to show improvement regardless of the added drug, and responders who had already lost ≥5% were measured for additional loss from an already-reduced weight. The comparison of 12-month weight loss from GLP-1 initiation (monotherapy) versus 12-month weight loss from bupropion/naltrexone add-on (combination) compares fundamentally different time windows and disease trajectories.
why_fatal: The unequal baseline time points and the responder/non-responder stratification based on prior GLP-1 response make the comparison invalid. The combination therapy group's outcomes are confounded by regression to the mean — non-responders would be expected to lose more weight over time regardless, and responders were already on a favorable trajectory. The monotherapy group's 12-month results from drug initiation cannot be meaningfully compared to the combination group's 12-month results from a later add-on date, as the groups are at different points in their treatment course. This immortal time bias (the combination group had to survive/persist on GLP-1 therapy long enough to receive the add-on) and regression to the mean together invalidate the conclusion that adding bupropion/naltrexone provides additive benefit.
correct_approach: The correct approach would have been to align baseline time points so that both groups are compared from the same reference point (e.g., GLP-1 initiation for both groups), or to use a time-matched/index-date matched design where the combination group's outcomes are compared to a comparable period of monotherapy. Regression to the mean should be addressed by not stratifying on prior response to define subgroups for outcome analysis, or by using appropriate statistical methods (e.g., mixed models with proper adjustment for pre-add-on weight trajectory). Ideally, a randomized controlled trial or a properly matched cohort with the same observation windows and accounting for immortal time bias should be used.
additional_errors: Immortal time bias: combination therapy patients had to remain on GLP-1 long enough to receive the add-on, systematically selecting for patients who tolerated and persisted with therapy
Regression to the mean in the responder/non-responder stratification, which guarantees apparent benefit in the non-responder subgroup
Use of hazard ratio (HR 0.88) reported in the results is unusual for a continuous weight loss outcome and may reflect inappropriate statistical modeling
Retrospective, non-randomized design with likely residual confounding (e.g., patients given combination therapy may differ systematically from monotherapy patients)
No adjustment for the duration of GLP-1 therapy before add-on of bupropion/naltrexone
